author: @meex88hiphop
text:
hello Robert, i would suggest one small but arguably better improvement:
printing a rotor so that the magnets are outside the stator, rather than inside, would increase the speed at which the magnets are passing over the wire and it would eliminate the chance of magnets flying off the rotor, due to centrifugal forces.

on the other hand, you could increase the power output density if you print multiple wire-holding rings, for example, in front and behind the magnets so both sides of the magnet are being exploited

author: @nhaduphot9303
text: Oh dear, yet another video demostrating the complete lack of understanding of magnetic flux.   Air (or plastic) has a magnetic resistance to magnetic flux of one thousand times that of iron.   So to be effective in generating useful power you must complete the flux path with iron, minimise the air (or plastic) gap between the rotating and the stationary parts and flatten the coils because they are essentially also an air gap.   Have a look at a good printed circuit motor to see that the coils are very flat (a mm or less) and the bulk of the flux path is either iron (or magnet).   While this design is novel, it is not practical.   Actually, it is even not that novel.   Take a look at the Fisher and Paykel washing machine motor and you will see that is is a similar layout (except with 3 overlapping phases).   Please make a dynamometer so you can test the POWER of these generators and motors and you will see how pathetic they are because of my abovementioned points.   3D plastic printing is great but it can make us lose sight of the principles.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lololol - I see you missed the point entirely

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: Wow! Wow!

replies.author: @nhaduphot9303
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering What was the point again ?   I think you missed the point in my comment, and that is - you can't make an efficient practical motor without including iron.   And the reason is - FLUX or more correctly the flux path.   Trying to just add iron to the serpent motor to make it better won't improve things much unless you understand the need to reduce the magnetic reluctance and increase the rate-of-change of magnetic flux.   The serpent design is indeed novel and it would seem that it makes it very easy to construct and place the coil but try making a real motor (or generator) this way - one that handles significant power and you will find that you need iron to complete the flux path.   Try making a small load for this design of yours and show just how little power it can generate.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Serpentine coils still suffer a  significant amount of non producing copper overhang in the direction of motion.

Let me demonstrate. 

Let's compare the serpentine coil to the faulhaber coil for contrast, and 
for simplicity let's use a magnitude of EMF generated as 1 cm × 1 tesla /1 second. And let's use  a 1cm×1cm magnet arrangement of 1 tesla magnets. With zero spacing between magnets.

The serpentine coil has a 
1cm segment of wire at 90 degree to the direction of motion and a 1cm section of wire at 0 degrees to the direction of motion. Sin 90 degrees = 1, and  sin 0 degrees = 0. When adding the emf of both sections we get... 
(1cm × 1 tesla / 1 second × 1)  + 
(1cm × 1 tesla / 1 second × 0) = an EMF of (1cm × tesla / 1 second)

Where as the same arrangement of magnets with a faulhaber coil having a 45 degrees diagonal equal to sqrt (1cm²+1cm²) = sqrt(2) or  1.41421cm aprox,  and the sin of 45 degrees = 0.707106 aprox

Thus
(1.41421cm  × 1 tesla / 1 second × 0.707106) = an EMF of 
(1 cm × 1 tesla / 1 second).

In short. Both coils produce the same 1cm × 1 tesla / 1 second  EMF but where the serpentine  coil used 2 cm of wire, the faulhaber coil used just 1.41421cm of wire. In other words, the faulhaber coil is  70.7106% that of the serpentine coil to produce the same EMF. 


The only EMF producing portion of a coil is the vector quantity of wire in the direction at 90 degrees to the direction of motion. Both the faulhaber coil and serpentine coil have the same vector quantity of wire at 90 degrees to the direction of motion thus produce the same EMF  . Its just thst the faulhaber coil takes the shorter distance between the center points of two magnets. 

The taller a magnet and  serpentine coil is with respect to the distance between center points of two adjacent magnets, the closer the serpentine approaches the length of wire for the emf produced  performance of a faulhabor.

author: @sumdumbmick
text:
the physics sense of 'vector' is strange, though.

honestly, virtually every mathematical object is a vector. you can't even add or subtract anything but vectors. the reason being that addition and subtraction are not even operations, they're just the names we give to listing vectors of either positive or negative polarity. so:
-> 3+4 = 4+3 because the list (+3, +4) = the list (+4, +3)
-> -3+4 = 4-3 because the list (-3, +4) = the list (+4, -3)

but note that in the case of -3+4 there is no 'subtraction' while in 4-3 there is but now there's no 'addition'. that's because the symbols we naively call the addition and subtraction operators are indicating the direction of the vector they're part of, not actually serving as mathematical operators.

you can try to argue this, but if you really look into it you'll quickly see that you can't. this is in fact what is really happening. addition and subtraction do not exist, only the summation of vectors does. and this ends up having rather significant consequences for the entirety of the philosophy of mathematics, which pretty much immediately invalidates most of what you're saying about 'vectors' and 'scalars', since it becomes necessary to recognize that all of the things you're calling 'scalars' are also vectors. and in fact even many things we call numbers, like 2/3 and pi, are in fact necessarily vectors.

replies.author: @sumdumbmick
replies.text:
you can see the 'right hand rule' in complex exponents. that makes absolutely no sense if reals are not vectors in the first place:

exponentiation of x^(j/u), for all integer j, all real u, and all complex x:

starting at 1:
  magnitude: multiply by x, j/u times.
  unit: u equally distributed solutions on the complex unit circle
        - the first solution occurs at 1/u * angle of x from 1
          - if this first solution is not on the positive real axis, then there is no solution on the positive real axis

caveats:
  if the exponent is complex then the distribution of the u solutions around the complex unit circle changes depending on x (this is an incomplete account, but insofar as it goes, accurate)
  - if the complex exponent is (j/u + i*k)
    - the starting point is rotated to ln(x)*k radians from the first solution
      - rotation is from positive reals toward positive imaginaries
      - the u solutions are all rotated by the same ln(x)*k radians
      - magnitudes are also inversely scaled by e^(h*pi*1/u) for all integer h
        - as the angle grows the magnitude shrinks (by the same amount)
          - these results fall along logarithmic spirals
        - this yields infinite solutions for all complex powers of any non-zero base
    - note this description is circular for including ln(x), and thus unacceptable as a definition, though it does complete the description of exponentiation of real and complex vectors

  this behavior extends at least to octonions, and perhaps to arbitrarily high dimensions.

replies.author: @sumdumbmick
replies.text:
for a real mind bender, consider what this means for 'addition' of zero.

-> 0+3 should be the same as 3+0, right? but the first one is the list (0, +3) while the second is the list (+3, +0)... what does +0 mean?

ultimately you should recognize that 'addition' of zero is actually undefined nonsense, while 'addition' to zero is perfectly fine. and this is a simple consequence of 0 not being a vector while everything else we use in math is.

The predecessor of this video, the flywheel flashlight, is a practical solution for emergency lighting.  It's better than the crank flashlight I bought.

If he scaled up e.g. stacking the savonius turbines (one of the easiest quickie projects) to make a 10x6 wall you'd have hundreds of hours of 3d printing, a large frame that would need to made of steel and with real bearings, many hundreds of dollars in magnets, and a couple of miles of wire to wind into coils.  Instead of a project that hundreds of people can do for $10 you get a project 1 person in a million might do.  Scaling up is hard.

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text:
In the language of science, the word "law" describes an analytic statement. It gives us a formula that tells us what things will do. For example, Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation tells us:

"Every point mass attracts every single point mass by a force pointing along the line intersecting both points. The force is directly proportional to the product of the two masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the point masses."

So if we know the mass of two objects, and the distance between the center of mass of the two objects, we can calculate the gravitational pull between the Earth and the object you dropped, between the Sun and Mars, or between me and a bowl of ice cream.

We can use Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation to calculate how strong the gravitational pull is between the Earth and the object you dropped, which would let us calculate its acceleration as it falls, how long it will take to hit the ground, how fast it would be going at impact, how much energy it will take to pick it up again, etc.

While the law lets us calculate quite a bit about what happens, notice that it does not tell us anything about WHY it happens. That is what theories are for. In the language of science, a theory is an explanation of why and how things happen. For gravity, we use Einstein's Theory of General Relativity to explain why things fall.

A theory starts as one or more hypotheses, untested ideas about why something happens. For example, I might propose a hypothesis that the object that you released fell because it was pulled by the Earth's magnetic field. Once we started testing, it would not take long to find out that my hypothesis was not supported by the evidence. Non-magnetic objects fall at the same rate as magnetic objects. Because it was not supported by the evidence, my hypothesis does not gain the status of being a theory. To become a scientific theory, an idea must be thoroughly tested, and must be an accurate and predictive description of the natural world.

While laws rarely change, theories change frequently as new evidence is discovered. Instead of being discarded because of new evidence, theories are often revised to include the new evidence in their explanation. The Theory of General Relativity has adapted as new technologies and new evidence have expanded our view of the universe.

So when we are scientifically discussing gravity, we can talk about the law of gravity that describes the attraction between two objects, and we can also talk about the theory of gravity that describes why the objects attract each other.
